title: Conversational + Clinical Spanish Foundations
description: 30 lessons toward clinical readiness AND conversational confidence with Spanish speakers. Mexican Spanish throughout.

lessons.title: Greetings & Introductions
lessons.subtitle: Hola, ¿cómo estás? + how to introduce yourself
objectives: Greet someone in three registers (formal, casual, slang)
Ask 'how are you' and respond naturally
lessons.warmup: Quick recap: today we'll practice three ways to greet — formal, casual, and slang.
lessons.practice: Roleplay: I'm someone you just met at a casual gathering. Greet me, introduce yourself (name + that you're studying nursing), then ask me about myself. Use a casual register.
lessons.teachBack: Now imagine you're teaching a friend who's never spoken Spanish how to introduce themselves. Walk me through what to say.

masteryCheck.target: Mucho gusto, soy Christian, soy estudiante de enfermería.

lessons.title: Numbers, Time, Dates
lessons.subtitle: Counting, telling time, days of the week
objectives: Tell time (it's 3:30, the appointment is at 9 AM)
lessons.warmup: We'll handle numbers up to 100 today, then tell time, then days/dates. This is foundational for scheduling, billing, and intake questions.
lessons.practice: Roleplay: I'm calling to schedule a clinic appointment. Ask me what day works, confirm a time, and read back the date in Spanish.
lessons.teachBack: Explain to a colleague how to ask 'when is your appointment?' and how to give a time-of-day answer.

masteryCheck.target: Tu cita es el viernes a las nueve y media de la mañana.

lessons.title: Asking Questions
lessons.subtitle: qué, dónde, cuándo, cómo, por qué, cuánto
objectives: Use the inverted ¿ correctly in writing
Choose the right interrogative for context
lessons.warmup: Question words are the swiss army knife of conversation. We'll practice each one in context.
lessons.practice: Roleplay: you're getting to know a new coworker. Ask me at least 5 questions about myself using different interrogatives. I'll respond with information you can follow up on.
lessons.teachBack: Walk me through the difference between '¿qué hora es?' and '¿a qué hora?' — when do you use each?

masteryCheck.target: ¿A qué hora empieza la cita?

lessons.title: Family & Personal Info
lessons.subtitle: Talking about your family, work, where you live
objectives: Talk about your job and studies
lessons.warmup: Family is sacred in Mexican culture. Knowing how to ask about and share family info builds rapport instantly.
lessons.practice: Roleplay: a Spanish-speaking patient's family member is making small talk while you wait. Ask about their family, share about yours, find common ground.
lessons.teachBack: How would you politely ask a patient if family is involved in their care decisions?

masteryCheck.target: Vivo en Bend con mi familia y estudio enfermería.

lessons.title: Getting Around
lessons.subtitle: Basic prepositions + 'hay' (there is/are)
objectives: Use prepositions: en, sobre, debajo, al lado de
lessons.warmup: 'Hay' is one of the highest-leverage words in Spanish — it lets you describe almost anything.
lessons.practice: Roleplay: a patient asks where the bathroom is, where the pharmacy is in the building, and how far the parking lot is. Give clear directions.
lessons.teachBack: Explain how 'hay' is different from 'es/está'.

masteryCheck.target: El baño está al lado de la sala de espera.

lessons.title: Ordering Food
lessons.subtitle: Taquería + restaurant survival
objectives: Order confidently in a Mexican restaurant
Ask for the bill and pay
lessons.warmup: Mexican food vocab is also a cultural passport — a few words in confident Spanish open doors.
lessons.practice: Roleplay: you're at a taquería in CDMX. I'm the taquero. Order 3 tacos al pastor con todo, an agua de jamaica, and ask for the bill.
lessons.teachBack: Tell me when to use 'me da' vs 'quisiera' vs 'quiero'.

masteryCheck.target: Me da tres de pastor con todo, por favor.

lessons.title: At the Market
lessons.subtitle: Shopping + haggling without being rude
lessons.warmup: Haggling in markets is expected and respectful when done right.
lessons.practice: Roleplay: an open-air market in Oaxaca. I'm the seller. Find something you like, ask the price, negotiate down 30%, and close the deal politely.
lessons.teachBack: How do you start a price negotiation without sounding rude?

masteryCheck.target: ¿Cuál es su último precio?

lessons.title: Asking Directions
lessons.subtitle: Getting around when you're lost
objectives: Politely interrupt a stranger to ask
lessons.warmup: Directions are a survival skill — and the way you ask sets the tone.
lessons.practice: Roleplay: you're lost in CDMX looking for the metro. Stop me politely, ask for directions, then read them back to confirm.
lessons.teachBack: Why is 'disculpe' better than 'perdón' to start asking?

masteryCheck.target: Disculpe, ¿cómo llego al metro más cercano?

lessons.title: Public Transit / Uber
lessons.subtitle: Getting from point A to point B
objectives: Make small talk during a ride
lessons.warmup: Uber drivers are some of the friendliest Spanish-language teachers you'll ever meet.
lessons.practice: Roleplay: I'm an Uber driver in CDMX, you've just gotten in. Confirm where we're going, make small talk, ask for restaurant recommendations.
lessons.teachBack: What's a friendly farewell to a stranger you'll never see again?

masteryCheck.target: ¿Me lleva a Polanco, por favor?

lessons.title: Phone Calls & Reservations
lessons.subtitle: Making appointments by phone (no body language)
objectives: Open and close a phone call professionally

vocab.en: good morning, this is X calling
I'd like to make a reservation

lessons.warmup: Phone Spanish is harder than in-person — you can't read faces. Confirm everything explicitly.
lessons.practice: Roleplay: you're calling a restaurant. Make a reservation for 4 people, tomorrow at 8 PM, in your name. Confirm by reading it back.
lessons.teachBack: What's the safest way to confirm you got a date and time right on the phone?

masteryCheck.target: Quisiera una reservación para cuatro personas a las ocho.

lessons.title: Patient Intake — Chief Complaint
lessons.subtitle: Opening the clinical interview
objectives: Greet a patient and introduce yourself professionally

vocab.en: I'm a nurse, my name is...

lessons.warmup: First impressions matter clinically — patients decide in 30 seconds whether they trust you.
lessons.practice: Roleplay: I'm a Mexican-American patient who has just walked into your clinic with abdominal pain. Greet me, introduce yourself, ask about the chief complaint, and gather a basic timeline. Use usted throughout.
lessons.teachBack: Walk me through the first 30 seconds of any patient encounter, in Spanish.

masteryCheck.target: Buenos días, soy Christian, soy enfermero. ¿Qué le trae hoy?

lessons.title: Pain Assessment
lessons.subtitle: OPQRST + descriptors patients actually use
objectives: Use the 0–10 pain scale in Spanish
Recognize patient pain descriptors (punzante, ardor, opresivo)
lessons.warmup: Patients describe pain expressively. 'Punzante' = sharp, 'ardor' = burning, 'opresivo' = squeezing. Older men may UNDERREPORT — anchor with 'siendo 10 el peor dolor de su vida'.
lessons.practice: Roleplay: I'm a 65-year-old man with chest pain. Conduct a full pain assessment — quality, severity, radiation, duration, what makes it worse/better. Push gently if I underreport.
lessons.teachBack: Teach me the OPQRST sequence in Spanish, with one example question for each letter.

masteryCheck.target: Del cero al diez, siendo diez el peor dolor de su vida, ¿cómo está su dolor ahora?

lessons.title: Allergies & Medications
lessons.subtitle: Med reconciliation safely

vocab.en: are you allergic to any medication?

vocab.es: qué le pasa cuando lo toma
vocab.en: what happens when you take it

lessons.warmup: Mexican patients often use 'remedios caseros' — herbal teas, ointments. Ask about them explicitly without judgment, they can interact with meds.
lessons.practice: Roleplay: a patient on multiple meds. Ask about allergies (with reactions), reconcile current meds with dosing schedules, and ask about home remedies.
lessons.teachBack: How do you ask about allergies AND make sure to capture the actual reaction (not just the drug name)?

masteryCheck.target: ¿Es alérgico a algún medicamento? ¿Qué le pasa cuando lo toma?

lessons.title: Vital Signs Teaching
lessons.subtitle: Explaining numbers without scaring patients
lessons.warmup: Patients want to know if their numbers are 'good'. Be honest but reassuring.
lessons.practice: Roleplay: a patient curious about vitals. Take BP/pulse/temp/SpO2 verbally, explain each number, address their question 'is this OK?'
lessons.teachBack: Walk me through how to soften a slightly elevated BP reading without minimizing it.

masteryCheck.target: Su presión está un poquito alta, pero no es preocupante.

lessons.title: Common Symptoms
lessons.subtitle: Vocabulary for the things patients describe
lessons.warmup: Symptom vocabulary is common-cold simple but mistakes here cost trust. Build muscle memory.
lessons.practice: Roleplay: a patient with nausea, dizziness, headache, and a low-grade fever for 2 days. Document each symptom's onset, severity, and frequency.
lessons.teachBack: How would you summarize 'fever for 2 days, intermittent, max 38.5°C' in Spanish back to a colleague?

masteryCheck.target: ¿Desde cuándo tiene el dolor de cabeza?

lessons.title: Usted vs. Tú in Clinical Settings
lessons.subtitle: Register matters — when to switch
objectives: Recognize when tú is appropriate (kids, peers, family of close patients)
Stay in usted in emergencies (avoid sounding condescending)
lessons.warmup: Default to usted with adults. With kids, tú is warm. In emergencies, KEEP usted — switching to tú can read as condescending.
lessons.practice: Roleplay scenarios: (1) elderly patient, (2) 8-year-old child, (3) patient your age — adjust register naturally.
lessons.teachBack: Explain to me when you'd switch from usted to tú with a patient.

masteryCheck.target: Permítame ayudarle. ¿Cómo se siente ahora?

lessons.title: Familismo & Family in Decisions
lessons.subtitle: Including la familia respectfully
objectives: Invite family into the conversation appropriately
Find privacy when needed for sensitive topics

vocab.en: would you like us to talk with your family?
give you time to discuss it

lessons.warmup: Familismo is real — family is often integral to decisions. ASK rather than assume privacy preferences.
lessons.practice: Roleplay: a patient with new diagnosis wanting family input. Navigate inclusion, decision-making, and a moment that needs privacy.
lessons.teachBack: How do you invite family in without removing the patient's agency?

masteryCheck.target: ¿Quiere que platiquemos con su familia, o prefiere decidir usted?

lessons.title: Mexican Slang Survival Kit
lessons.subtitle: 20 words you'll hear daily
objectives: Know which to AVOID in clinical settings

vocab.en: right now / in a bit (Mexican fuzzy)

lessons.warmup: Slang opens doors socially but use carefully clinically. Today: 20 high-leverage words, what they mean, when to use.
lessons.practice: Casual chat: I'll use slang naturally, you respond and try to mirror back. We'll keep it casual.
lessons.teachBack: Pick three slang words and tell me a context where each is appropriate.

masteryCheck.target: No manches, qué chido. ¿Neta?

lessons.title: Reading Patient Stoicism
lessons.subtitle: When 'estoy bien' doesn't mean 'I'm fine'
objectives: Recognize cultural stoicism in pain reports
Use anchoring language to get accurate reports

vocab.es: siendo el peor dolor de su vida
vocab.en: being the worst pain of your life

lessons.warmup: Mexican patients (esp. older men) may underreport pain due to machismo or stoicism. Anchor and probe — gently.
lessons.practice: Roleplay: a stoic 60-year-old who's clearly in significant pain but says 'estoy bien'. Probe gently and ethically.
lessons.teachBack: Why is 'siendo 10 el peor dolor de su vida' a stronger anchor than just '0 to 10'?

masteryCheck.target: Platíqueme más. No se aguante, dígame qué tan fuerte es.

lessons.title: Indigenous-language Patients & Interpreters
lessons.subtitle: When Spanish is also a 2nd language
objectives: Recognize when Spanish is the patient's 2nd language
lessons.warmup: Central Oregon has Mixteco, Triqui, and Zapoteco speakers from Oaxaca. For them, Spanish is also a 2nd language. ASK before assuming.
lessons.practice: Roleplay: a Mixteco speaker with limited Spanish. Confirm preferred language, set up a phone interpreter, conduct intake through them.
lessons.teachBack: What three things do you need to document about an interpreter encounter?

masteryCheck.target: ¿Habla usted español, o prefiere otro idioma?

lessons.title: Medication Teaching with Teach-Back
lessons.subtitle: Confirming patient understanding
objectives: Explain dosing, frequency, food interactions clearly

vocab.es: con comida / con el estómago vacío
explíqueme cómo lo va a tomar
vocab.en: tell me how you'll take it

lessons.warmup: Mexican patients often don't ask follow-up questions out of respect — proactively ask 'qué dudas tiene' AND use teach-back.
lessons.practice: Roleplay: prescribing metoprolol BID with food. Explain it, then ASK ME to teach it back to you. Correct anything I miss.
lessons.teachBack: Walk me through medication teaching for any drug, with explicit teach-back.

masteryCheck.target: Por favor, explíqueme cómo va a tomar esta pastilla.

lessons.title: Procedures Explained Simply
lessons.subtitle: IV, blood draw, EKG, CT scan
objectives: Explain procedures in plain Spanish (avoid jargon)
Set expectations for what they'll feel

vocab.es: le voy a poner una vía
vocab.en: I'm going to start an IV

lessons.warmup: Patients value being TOLD what to expect. 'Le voy a poner una vía' beats 'le voy a canalizar' every time.
lessons.practice: Roleplay: a patient who's never had an IV. Explain step-by-step, set expectations, reassure.
lessons.teachBack: What's the right order: explain, ask consent, reassure — or some other?

masteryCheck.target: Le voy a poner una vía. Va a sentir un piquete pequeño.

lessons.title: Discharge Instructions
lessons.subtitle: Sending the patient home with what they need
objectives: Cover follow-up, prescription pickup, return precautions
Use teach-back for return precautions especially

vocab.es: lo vamos a dar de alta

vocab.en: do you have a way to fill your prescription

lessons.warmup: Discharge is the highest-stakes teaching moment. Confirm transportation, support, prescription access. Don't assume.
lessons.practice: Roleplay: discharging after minor surgery. Cover wound care, follow-up, warning signs, prescription, transportation. Teach-back the warning signs.
lessons.teachBack: Walk me through a discharge. Hit all 5 things.

masteryCheck.target: Regrese inmediatamente si presenta fiebre alta o sangrado.

lessons.title: Mental Health Screening
lessons.subtitle: PHQ-2/9 + suicide risk in Spanish

vocab.en: have you thought about harming yourself?

vocab.es: le pregunto a todos mis pacientes
esto es entre usted y yo
vocab.en: this is between you and me

lessons.warmup: Mental health is heavily stigmatized in many Mexican communities — 'estar nervioso' or 'tener los nervios' may be how depression presents. Normalize. Find privacy.
lessons.practice: Roleplay: a 45-year-old male, possibly depressed but not naming it. Run PHQ-2 with normalization framing. If positive, escalate to PHQ-9 + suicide question with privacy.
lessons.teachBack: How do you ask the suicide-risk question without making the patient feel singled out?

masteryCheck.target: Le pregunto a todos mis pacientes: ¿ha pensado en hacerse daño?

lessons.title: Substance Use History
lessons.subtitle: Alcohol, tobacco, drugs — without shame
objectives: Ask about substance use without judgment

vocab.es: le pregunto a todos mis pacientes

lessons.warmup: Skipping substance-use questions is common but yields bad clinical data. Normalize, ask without judgment, probe past 'social drinker'.
lessons.practice: Roleplay: a 50-year-old construction worker. Ask alcohol/tobacco/recreational drugs. Probe past first answer to quantify accurately.
lessons.teachBack: How do you push past a vague 'a couple beers' to get a real number?

masteryCheck.target: Le pregunto a todos mis pacientes — ¿toma alcohol? ¿Con qué frecuencia?

lessons.title: Pediatric Parent Interview
lessons.subtitle: Talking with parents about their child
objectives: Address the parent in usted; the child warmly

vocab.en: since when has [child] had a fever?

lessons.warmup: Mexican families often arrive together. The mom answers, but abuela may know vaccination history better. Welcome the network.
lessons.practice: Roleplay: a mother with a 2-year-old, fever for 24h. Conduct intake. The grandmother knows the vaccination history — invite her in.
lessons.teachBack: How do you respectfully include a grandmother in a peds intake?

masteryCheck.target: ¿Tiene la cartilla de vacunas con usted?

lessons.title: Labor & Delivery Basics
lessons.subtitle: Contractions, water broke, pain coping
objectives: Recognize labor terminology (contracciones, los dolores, fuente)
Respect cultural birth preferences without judgment
lessons.warmup: Patients often say 'los dolores' for contractions. Some refuse epidural for cultural reasons — explore without judgment.
lessons.practice: Roleplay: 38-week primip in active labor, contractions every 4 min. Coach breathing, respect epidural decision either way.
lessons.teachBack: How do you coach pushing in Spanish without barking commands?

masteryCheck.target: Respire profundo conmigo. Cuando le diga, puje.

lessons.title: Difficult Conversations
lessons.subtitle: Code status, family meetings, end of life
objectives: Discuss code status without using death directly
lessons.warmup: End-of-life conversations are deeply cultural. Avoid direct death-talk if family hasn't initiated. Allow silence. Use 'si su salud empeorara'.
lessons.practice: Roleplay: adult daughter of terminal cancer patient. You're explaining palliative care options. Hold the emotion. Don't fill silence.
lessons.teachBack: Why does silence matter in this conversation?

masteryCheck.target: Lo lamento mucho. Estoy aquí para escucharle.

lessons.title: OHP / Insurance Navigation
lessons.subtitle: Oregon Health Plan, mixed-status families, confidentiality
objectives: Reassure on confidentiality without being asked
Know what OHP covers regardless of status

vocab.es: no le pregunto sobre su estatus
vocab.en: I'm not asking about your status

lessons.warmup: Many Oregon patients are eligible for OHP regardless of status — but they don't know it. Lead with reassurance.
lessons.practice: Roleplay: undocumented mom-to-be, 38, hesitant on forms. Reassure on confidentiality, status, OHP coverage for prenatal care.
lessons.teachBack: What's the first thing you should say to a hesitant patient about insurance forms?

masteryCheck.target: No le pregunto sobre su estatus. Esto es confidencial.

lessons.title: Capstone — Full Clinical Interview
lessons.subtitle: Putting it all together
objectives: Conduct a full intake from greeting to discharge
Sustain a 10+ minute clinical conversation in Spanish

vocab.en: how can I help you today?
let me repeat what I understood
you have my number if you need

lessons.warmup: This is the culminating session. We'll do a full clinical interview — intake to discharge — entirely in Spanish.
lessons.practice: Roleplay: walk-in patient, fictional case (you'll get details). Conduct: greeting, chief complaint, history, allergies, meds, vitals teaching, plan, discharge with teach-back. ~15 minutes.
lessons.teachBack: Reflect on the session: what felt most natural? Where did you struggle? What's the next concept to drill?

masteryCheck.target: Cuídese mucho. Tiene mi número si necesita.
